intro_text:
Hanakapiʻai Beach sits on the Nā Pali Coast of northwest Kauaʻi, at 22.209 N, 159.598 W, two miles along the Kalalau Trail from Keʻe Beach in Hāʻena State Park. No road reaches it. The only ways in are that two-mile trail, which climbs and falls roughly 1,200 feet along the cliff face, or a boat landing in calm summer water. There are no lifeguards, no facilities, no fresh water, and no reliable cell service.

The beach is seasonal. During the summer months the sand is exposed and the cove reads as a strand. In winter, powerful waves and high tides strip the sand away and scour the cove down to boulders and bare rock. The Open-Meteo Marine reanalysis records an annual mean wave height of about 2.05 meters here, rising to a January mean near 3.21 meters and a recorded big-day height of 4.68 meters. This is a heavy, surf-driven coast.

What the beach is known for is the dying. A wooden sign near the shore tallies drowning victims; tourists have counted 82 marks as of August 2014, a figure the beach's own documentation calls unofficial and speculative. A more conservative, sourced count holds that about 30 people are known to have drowned here between 1970 and 2010, and that at least 15 bodies have never been recovered. The Nā Pali Coast has no major reefs to break the swell, so currents run unhindered. Anyone swept off the beach faces a nearest safe shore roughly six miles away. There is no exit and no rescue in the minutes a drowning takes.

Hanakapiʻai Stream crosses the back of the beach and drains the valley behind it. Heavy rain inland, which a hiker on the beach cannot see, can send a flash flood down the stream within minutes, cutting off the crossing and trapping people on the far side. The same stream feeds Hanakapiʻai Falls, a roughly 300-foot waterfall two miles further inland up an unmaintained, repeatedly stream-crossing trail.

Since the 2018 north-shore floods washed out the road and damaged Hāʻena State Park, access has been rebuilt around a reservation system. After the park reopened in 2019, day hikers must reserve parking and entry in advance through the state's gohaena.com system; arriving without a reservation means being turned away at the gate. The reservation governs the only legal land approach to the trailhead.

This is a hazard page, not an invitation. The beach is the destination of one of Hawaiʻi's most dangerous day hikes, and its defining feature is a count of the people the water has taken.
honest_reckoning_note: The sign is the honest part. Most beaches sell an image; Hanakapiʻai keeps a tally. The exact number on the wooden marker is disputed — visitors have read 82 tally marks, the beach's own documentation calls that figure unofficial, and the better-sourced count is about 30 known drownings from 1970 to 2010 with at least 15 bodies never recovered. The dispute over the number does not soften the fact it records: people die here, and they die because the conditions are built to kill. There is no reef to break the Nā Pali swell, the nearest safe shore is roughly six miles off, and there are no lifeguards on a beach two miles from the nearest road. The water looks survivable on a calm summer afternoon, which is exactly the trap. The same valley that draws hikers to Hanakapiʻai Falls also drains flash floods down the stream that crosses the beach, and people have been stranded and killed by water that arrived from rain they never saw. The sober reading is that the beach is worth reaching and not worth entering — that the correct relationship to this stretch of the Pacific is to stay out of it.

day_in_time.dawn: The first hikers leave Keʻe Beach at first light to make the two miles before the heat, while the cove is still in the shadow of the valley walls and the surf shows its overnight size. There are no lights on the trail; hiking before dawn is unsanctioned and the trail officially opens at sunrise.
day_in_time.midday: By late morning the beach holds its day-trip crowd: people who hiked in, ate, photographed the cliffs and the warning sign, and most of whom do not enter the water. In summer the sand is exposed and the stream crossing is ankle-deep; the falls-bound continue two more miles inland.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon light rakes the green valley walls and the boulders at the waterline while the lower beach falls into shade. This is the turnaround deadline: hikers must clear the two-mile trail before dark, when cliff exposure and loose footing become lethal.
day_in_time.night: After sunset the beach is empty and unlit, with no facilities, no staff, and no one permitted to stay without an overnight Kalalau camping permit obtained separately. The trail is closed to night hiking; the only presence is the surf working the cove and, on clear nights, an unusually dark sky over the wilderness coast.

food_drink.name: Nothing at the beach
food_drink.description: Hanakapiʻai has no kiosk, taverna, shade structure, or potable water. Hikers must carry all food and at least three liters of water in and pack all trash out. The stream is not safe to drink and can carry leptospirosis.
food_drink.where: Plan around the trail, not the beach

food_drink.name: Provisioning in Hanalei
food_drink.description: The last real town before the road ends, Hanalei has grocery stores and restaurants where hikers buy supplies for the trail. It is roughly 18 miles southeast of the Hāʻena trailhead on Kūhiō Highway.

timeline.title: Modern drowning record begins
timeline.description: The sourced count of Hanakapiʻai drownings dates from about 1970; roughly 30 people are known to have drowned here between 1970 and 2010, with at least 15 bodies never recovered.

timeline.title: Warning sign counted at 82 marks
timeline.description: As of August 2014, visitors counted 82 tally marks on the wooden warning sign near the beach, each representing a drowning victim. The figure is widely repeated but described as unofficial and speculative.

timeline.title: North-shore floods wash out access
timeline.description: Record April 2018 rainfall on Kauaʻi's north shore caused catastrophic flooding and landslides, washing out the road to Hāʻena and closing the state park and the Kalalau Trail trailhead.
timeline.source: Hawaiʻi DLNR; Hāʻena State Park notices

timeline.title: Park reopens with reservation system
timeline.description: Hāʻena State Park reopened in June 2019 after repairs, introducing a mandatory advance reservation and shuttle system for parking and entry, managed through the state's gohaena.com platform.
timeline.source: Hawaiʻi DLNR Division of State Parks; gohaena.com

zones.name: The seasonal cove
zones.character: A roughly one-mile crescent at the foot of the Nā Pali valley. Sand is exposed in summer and scoured to boulders and bare rock by winter surf and high tides. No reef offshore to break the swell.
zones.best_for: Reaching the Pacific on foot, photography, the turnaround point
zones.notes: No lifeguards, no facilities. Swimming is dangerous year-round; the nearest safe shore if swept out is about six miles away.

zones.name: The warning sign
zones.character: A wooden marker near the shore tallying drowning victims, read by visitors at 82 marks as of August 2014. The defining object of the beach.
zones.best_for: Understanding the actual hazard before deciding anything
zones.notes: The tally is unofficial; the sourced count is about 30 known drownings 1970–2010, at least 15 unrecovered.

zones.name: Hanakapiʻai Stream crossing
zones.character: The stream that drains the valley and crosses the back of the beach. Ankle-deep in dry conditions; it drives the falls-bound trail inland and can flash-flood within minutes after upstream rain.
zones.notes: Do not drink the water (leptospirosis risk). A rising stream can trap hikers on the far side; turn back if rain threatens.

zones.name: Kalalau Trail, miles 0–2
zones.character: The two-mile cliff-edge approach from Keʻe Beach, climbing and dropping about 1,200 feet over roots, loose rock, and exposed switchbacks with long drops to the sea.
zones.best_for: The only land access to the beach
zones.notes: Open sunrise to sunset; night hiking prohibited. Requires advance Hāʻena State Park reservation via gohaena.com.

landmarks.name: Hanakapiʻai drowning warning sign
landmarks.description: A wooden sign near the beach tallying drowning deaths with carved marks, counted by visitors at 82 as of August 2014. It is the beach's most recognized feature and the subject of most of its photographs.

landmarks.name: Hanakapiʻai Falls
landmarks.description: A waterfall of roughly 300 feet (visible drop) at the head of the valley, two miles inland from the beach up an unmaintained trail with repeated stream crossings. The full drop is reported higher, with about 300 feet visible from the valley floor.

landmarks.name: Nā Pali Coast cliffs
landmarks.description: The fluted volcanic sea cliffs of Kauaʻi's northwest coast, rising thousands of feet, that enclose the Hanakapiʻai valley and make the beach reachable only on foot or by sea.

cultural_refs.title: The counted-dead warning sign
cultural_refs.description: The tally sign has become the symbol of the beach in travel media and photography, repeated as shorthand for the danger of the Nā Pali Coast. The 82-mark count circulated widely despite being unofficial.

cultural_refs.title: Nā Pali Coast on film
cultural_refs.description: The Nā Pali cliffs and valleys, including the wilderness around Hanakapiʻai, have appeared as backdrop in feature films shot on Kauaʻi's north shore, contributing to the coast's image as untouched wilderness.

things_to_know.label: Reservation required
things_to_know.note: Hāʻena State Park parking and entry require an advance reservation via gohaena.com; without one you are turned away at the gate. Day hiking to Hanakapiʻai needs no separate permit, but overnight Kalalau camping does.

things_to_know.label: Do not swim
things_to_know.note: No reef breaks the swell, there are no lifeguards, and the nearest safe shore if swept out is about six miles away. A sign tallies the drownings; treat the water as off-limits.

things_to_know.label: Flash-flood risk
things_to_know.note: Hanakapiʻai Stream can rise within minutes after rain you cannot see upstream, cutting off the crossing. Turn back if rain threatens and do not drink the stream (leptospirosis).

things_to_know.label: The hike
things_to_know.note: Two miles each way from Keʻe Beach, about 1,200 feet of cumulative gain over exposed cliff trail. Open sunrise to sunset; carry at least three liters of water; the falls add four miles round trip on an unmaintained trail.

access_note: No road reaches the beach. Land access is the first two miles of the Kalalau Trail from Keʻe Beach in Hāʻena State Park, which requires an advance reservation via gohaena.com. Summer-only boat landings are possible in calm water. Nearest town for supplies is Hanalei; nearest commercial airport is Līhuʻe (LIH), about 40 miles south.
spike_explainer:
Most beaches are sold on what they show you. Hanakapiʻai is defined by what it counts. Near the shore stands a wooden sign with carved tally marks, one for each person the water is said to have taken, and that sign — not the sand, not the cliffs — is the thing the beach is known for. It is a hazard memorial standing on the destination itself.

**The number is contested, and the contest is the point.** Visitors have read 82 tally marks on the sign as of August 2014, and that figure travels widely. The beach's own documentation flags it as unofficial and speculative — marks can be added by anyone, and no agency maintains the sign as a record. The more defensible, sourced count is narrower and still grim: roughly 30 people known to have drowned here between 1970 and 2010, with at least 15 bodies never recovered. Whether the true toll is 30 or 82, both numbers describe the same fact. People come to this beach and do not leave it.

**Why the water actually kills.** The danger is structural, not incidental. The Nā Pali Coast has no major fringing reef to absorb the Pacific swell, so the surf and the currents arrive at the beach at full strength. The Open-Meteo Marine reanalysis puts the annual mean wave height near 2.05 meters, climbing to a January mean of about 3.21 meters and a recorded peak day of 4.68 meters. There are no lifeguards, because the beach is two miles from the nearest road and there is no way to staff it. And if a swimmer is pulled off the beach, the nearest safe shore is about six miles away along a cliff coast with no landing. A rip current here is not a scare; it is a one-way trip.

**The seasonal trap.** The beach changes with the season in a way that misleads people. In summer the sand is exposed, the surf eases, and the cove looks like a place you could swim. In winter, powerful waves and high tides strip the sand off entirely and scour the cove down to boulders and bare rock. The calm summer face is the dangerous one, because it is the one that invites entry. The water is never safe; it only sometimes looks safe.

**The valley adds its own hazard.** Hanakapiʻai Stream crosses the back of the beach and drains the valley that rises behind it toward Hanakapiʻai Falls, a roughly 300-foot waterfall two miles inland. That stream is the second killer. Rain falling in the high valley — rain a hiker standing on the beach cannot see — can send a flash flood down the channel within minutes, cutting off the crossing and stranding people on the wrong side. The same drainage that makes the falls hike possible makes the beach a place you can be trapped.

**Access enforces the seriousness.** You cannot stumble onto Hanakapiʻai. The only land route is the first two miles of the Kalalau Trail from Keʻe Beach, climbing and dropping about 1,200 feet along exposed cliff with long drops to the sea, open only between sunrise and sunset. Since the 2018 north-shore floods washed out the road and forced a 2019 reopening under a reservation system, even reaching the trailhead requires booking parking and entry in advance through the state's gohaena.com platform. Every layer of getting here — the reservation, the cliff hike, the daylight-only window — is a filter that the beach's own danger justifies. The sign at the end of the walk is the honest summary of everything the trail asked of you: this far, and no farther into the water.

key_facts.label: Location
key_facts.value: Nā Pali Coast, NW Kauaʻi (22.209 N, 159.598 W)

key_facts.label: Land access
key_facts.value: 2 miles on the Kalalau Trail from Keʻe Beach

key_facts.label: Nearest safe shore
key_facts.value: ~6 miles if swept out; no reef to break the swell

key_facts.label: Seasonal sand
key_facts.value: Sand in summer; scoured to boulders in winter

key_facts.label: Access reservation
key_facts.value: Hāʻena State Park entry via gohaena.com (post-2019)
key_facts.source: Hawaiʻi DLNR Division of State Parks

margin_notes.text: There are no lifeguards anywhere on this beach. It sits two miles from the nearest road by cliff trail, with no cell service you can rely on. Help is not minutes away; in a drowning, which takes two to three minutes, it is not coming at all.

The Nā Pali Coast has no major fringing reef. On most Hawaiian beaches a reef breaks the swell offshore; here the full Pacific arrives at the sand, which is why summer sand is scoured to bare boulders by winter surf.

The disputed count — 82 marks read on the sign versus ~30 sourced drownings 1970–2010 — matters less than the unrecovered figure: at least 15 bodies never found. The six-mile gap to safe shore is why.

The flash-flood danger comes from rain you cannot see. The valley draining toward Hanakapiʻai Falls catches storms high inland; the stream can rise from ankle-deep to impassable in minutes, stranding hikers on the far bank.

The April 2018 north-shore floods washed out the road to Hāʻena and shut the trailhead. The 2019 reopening built the current reservation gate at gohaena.com — the reason you now book entry weeks ahead.

reckoning_pullquote: Most beaches sell an image; Hanakapiʻai keeps a tally.
